Quartz Review - Empires & Puzzles Hero Strategy Guide

Remember when a 5-tile cleanse was revolutionary? Quartz brings the speed of a ninja and the fragility of a faberge egg. We dive deep into whether his 'Incite Ki' special can still keep your team alive or if he's just a purple participation trophy in the age of 3000 HP monsters. Spoiler: It involves a lot of emblems.
Quartz: The Ninja of Clean Slates & Dirty Diapers
Quartz: The Ninja of Clean Slates (and Fragile States)

Quartz arrived in late 2021 as part of the second wave of the Ninja Tower, bringing the signature "Charge" mana speed to the support role. As a Dark (Purple) 5-star Cleric, his claim to fame was—and still is—his incredible flexibility. Need a heal
? Done. Need a massive undispellable recovery later? He can do that too.

However, time is a cruel mistress in Empires & Puzzles. While his kit is mechanically sound, his stat line comes from an era when 800 defense was considered "tanky." This review explores if his utility can outrun his age.

Quartz sports the classic Ninja aesthetic: a hooded purple gi, face mask, and a massive spiked club that looks like it weighs more than he does. The art captures a "silent but deadly" vibe, though given his role is healing and cleansing, maybe "silent but helpful" is more accurate. It's a solid, if standard, design that fits perfectly within the Ninja family visual identity.
Low by modern standards, though less critical for a healer.
This is the danger zone. Modern 5-star heroes often launch with 1300+ Defense and 2400+ Health

Even with a double Limit Break (LB2), Quartz reaches stats (Defense ~955, HP ~1882) that are merely "okay" for a regular maxed modern hero. He is fragile and relies heavily on his family bonus and emblems to survive the nuclear blasts of today's special skills.
The beauty of Quartz is the
. You aren't stuck with one speed.

This is his MVP move. A 5-tile Cleanse + Attack Buff is incredibly rare. If you're facing a defense heavy on ailments (Mindless Attack, Defense Down), Quartz can reset the board state almost instantly. The heal is negligible (164 HP over 2 turns), but the utility is top-tier.

The "Rigard Zone." He heals 624 HP over 3 turns and gives +44% attack. Ironically, this is often considered his weakest mode because Costume Rigard does a very similar job with similar stats but better availability.
is fantastic, but charging 15 tiles on a hero with 774 Defense is a gamble. If he survives long enough to fire this, he can save the match.
5 tiles (Requires virtually no mana troops to hit 5 tiles, but 5 tiles is a weird breakpoint—usually you match 3 or 4, so you often need two matches regardless).
10 tiles. Comparable to Average speed heroes.
15 tiles. Comparable to Very Slow heroes.
Modern healers often boost health instantly by 600-1000. Quartz's Heal over Time (HoT) is slower and risky because dead heroes don't regenerate.
Family Bonus, Passives, Aether Power & Unique Abilities
The Ninja family bonus is one of the best in the game. A chance to
is huge for a squishy hero like Quartz. If you pair him with another Ninja (like Cobalt or Ruby), that dodge chance increases, which effectively boosts his survivability.

Unlike the newer Tower heroes, the original Ninjas (and their second wave additions like Quartz) do not have extra passive skills beyond the family bonus. This hurts him in the modern meta where passives often define a hero's viability.
. At the start of the battle, he gets a defense buff for 6 turns. This is actually
for him. It helps patch his biggest weakness (low defense) right when he is most vulnerable, buying him time to get his first charge off.
Upon release, Quartz was met with mixed reviews. Many compared him unfavorably to
, questioning why a 5-star hero was performing a 4-star's job. However, high-level players recognized the power of a 5-tile cleanse in War Attack teams.
The stat gap is massive. A modern sniper can one-shot Quartz without breaking a sweat. His buffs (+34% to +54% Attack) are also lower than modern bards or dedicated buffers who push +60% or more.

He is no longer a defense candidate. On offense, he is a "Specialist." You bring him when you specifically need to cleanse a fast ailment (like a tank's defense down) immediately. For general healing, modern healers vastly outperform him.
Note: At the time of this writing, Quartz does not have a costume in the live game. This review focuses on the base version.
Quartz is a support hero with paper-thin armor. There is only one logical path.
Maximizes his chances of surviving a slash attack or a stray special skill. The Cleric Manashield talent also benefits from him staying alive to resist mana cuts.
His tile damage will be low, but that's not his job.
. Quartz doesn't deal damage with his special, and his base attack is too low to make him a tile-damage titan. His value is entirely in firing his skill; he can't do that if he's dead.
Can act as a fast cleanser or a slow heavy healer depending on the board.
One of the few heroes who can cleanse the entire team with just 5 tiles.
The 3x charge healing cannot be wiped by standard dispellers.
The Ninja dodge can be a game-saver.
Severely low defense and health compared to the 2025/2026 meta.
His 2x charge is arguably worse than Costume Rigard (a 4-star).
In a meta of massive alpha-strikes, waiting for HP to tick up over turns is dangerous.
He cannot save a hero on the brink of death instantly; he can only start the recovery process.
Specifically against ailment-heavy defenses (e.g., Waterpipe, fast DoT dealers).
His flexibility shines here where you can manage his charge levels carefully.
He provides two buffs (Attack + HoT) at very fast speed.
Sergei drops Elemental Defense, Quartz cleanses any blind/attack down and buffs Attack, and the hitter cleans up. Quartz's 5-tile speed keeps up with the fastest heroes.
Maximizes the Ninja family dodge chance. Quartz keeps the heavy hitters (Ruby/Cobalt) clean of ailments so they can fire their devastating charges.
For his 1x and 2x charges, any dispeller wipes his buffs.
If blocked, he does literally nothing except a small cleanse.
Be careful not to overwrite a strong Heal Over Time from another hero with Quartz's weak 1x charge HoT.

If you pull Quartz early, he is excellent. He functions like a super-powered Rigard. Level him, but prioritize his defense in emblems. He will carry you through map stages and early raids because of his versatility.
Quartz is a luxury tool, not a staple. He is worth ascending

if you lack a very fast cleanser or if you are building a specific Ninja family attack team. Do not use Alpha Aethers on him unless you are swimming in them; his base stats are too low to yield a high return on investment compared to a modern hero.
"He is a 5-star version of C. Rigard... only his 3rd charge is better."
— This sentiment haunted his release. Players realized early on that charging 10 tiles for Quartz felt less rewarding than just using the trusty 4-star Rigard.
"Fastest cleanse possible in the game can’t be meh."
— The counter-argument. High-level play is often decided in the first few turns, and cleansing a defense down before the enemy sniper fires is worth more than raw healing numbers.
"Why does he heal for less than Rigard on 2x?"
— A common math complaint. It seems the "Ninja Tax" for flexibility was paid in raw output numbers.

Quartz is a relic of a more civilized age, where 5 tiles got you a cleanse and a pat on the back. In today's chaotic battlefield of mega-minions and 3000 HP stat-checks, he feels like bringing a katana to a drone strike.

However, utility is eternal. As long as "Defense Down" is a death sentence, a hero who can wipe it away in 5 tiles will always have a seat on the bench—even if he's too scared to step onto the field without a bodyguard.
Keep one for the toolbox, but don't expect him to tank the apocalypse.
Is Quartz still viable in 2026? A detailed review of the Dark Ninja hero, analyzing his 5-tile cleanse, charge mechanics, and how he holds up against modern power creep.
